infobox.key: Karl Theodor Anton Maria von Dalberg
infobox.value: (8 February 1744 – 10 February 1817) was a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") German [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") and [statesman](/wiki/Statesman_(politician) "Statesman (politician)"). In various capacities, he served as [Archbishop of Mainz](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Mainz "Archbishop of Mainz"), [Prince of Regensburg](/wiki/Principality_of_Regensburg "Principality of Regensburg"), [Arch-Chancellor](/wiki/Prince-Elector#High_offices "Prince-Elector") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), Bishop of [Constance](/wiki/Diocese_of_Constance "Diocese of Constance") and [Worms](/wiki/Diocese_of_Worms "Diocese of Worms"), [Prince-Primate](/wiki/Prince-Primate "Prince-Primate") of the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine")[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) and [Grand Duke](/wiki/Grand_Duke "Grand Duke") of [Frankfurt](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Frankfurt "Grand Duchy of Frankfurt"). Dalberg was the last Archbishop-Elector of Mainz.

([Free Imperial City](/wiki/Free_Imperial_City "Free Imperial City")) of [Wetzlar](/wiki/Wetzlar "Wetzlar") (with the rank of a Countship) and the [Principality of Regensburg](/wiki/Principality_of_Regensburg "Principality of Regensburg") containing the Imperial City, the [prince-bishopric](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Regensburg "Prince-Bishopric of Regensburg"), and some independent monasteries. (Regensburg was also where the Imperial Deputation had taken place.) In addition, he was designated Archbishop of the (former [Salzburg](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Salzburg "Archdiocese of Salzburg") suffragan) Regensburg,[[4]](#cite_note-tbm-4) to which (spiritually now) the former Mainz lands on the right bank of the Rhine, and the former Mainzian suffragans were attached.

**Karl Theodor Anton Maria von Dalberg** (8 February 1744 – 10 February 1817) was a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") German [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") and [statesman](/wiki/Statesman_(politician) "Statesman (politician)"). In various capacities, he served as [Archbishop of Mainz](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Mainz "Archbishop of Mainz"), [Prince of Regensburg](/wiki/Principality_of_Regensburg "Principality of Regensburg"), [Arch-Chancellor](/wiki/Prince-Elector#High_offices "Prince-Elector") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), Bishop of [Constance](/wiki/Diocese_of_Constance "Diocese of Constance") and [Worms](/wiki/Diocese_of_Worms "Diocese of Worms"), [Prince-Primate](/wiki/Prince-Primate "Prince-Primate") of the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine")[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) and [Grand Duke](/wiki/Grand_Duke "Grand Duke") of [Frankfurt](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Frankfurt "Grand Duchy of Frankfurt"). Dalberg was the last Archbishop-Elector of Mainz.

## Early life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karl_Theodor_Anton_Maria_von_Dalberg&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Vase_MET_DP102637.jpg/250px-Vase_MET_DP102637.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vase_MET_DP102637.jpg)

[Fürstenberg vase](/wiki/F%C3%BCrstenberg_China "Fürstenberg China") commemorating Dalberg's election in 1787 as Coadjutor of Mainz and Worms (Collections of the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"))

Born in [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"),[[2]](#cite_note-Bischof-2) as a member of the [House of Dalberg](/wiki/Dalberg "Dalberg"), he was the son of [Franz Heinrich von Dalberg](/w/index.php?title=Franz_Heinrich_von_Dalberg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Franz Heinrich von Dalberg (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franz_Heinrich_von_Dalberg "de:Franz Heinrich von Dalberg")] (1716–1776), administrator of Worms, one of the chief counsellors of the [Prince-elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") and [Archbishop of Mainz](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Mainz "Archbishopric of Mainz") and his wife, Baroness Maria Sophie Anna von [Eltz-Kempenich](/wiki/Eltz "Eltz") (1722–1763). Karl devoted himself to the study of [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law_(Catholic_Church) "Canon law (Catholic Church)"), and entered the church. At the beginning of 1765 he entered the administrative service of the ministry in Mainz.

Having been appointed in 1772 governor of [Erfurt](/wiki/Erfurt "Erfurt"), he won further advancement by his successful administration.[[3]](#cite_note-Galway-3) He was rector of the cathedral school in [Würzburg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrzburg "Würzburg") in 1780.[[2]](#cite_note-Bischof-2)

In 1787 he was elected [coadjutor cum iure successionis](/wiki/Coadjutor_bishop "Coadjutor bishop") of the [Archbishopric of Mainz](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Mainz "Archbishopric of Mainz") and the [Bishopric of Worms](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Worms "Bishopric of Worms"), and in 1788 of the [Bishopric of Constance](/wiki/Diocese_of_Constance "Diocese of Constance");[[4]](#cite_note-tbm-4) at the same time, he became [titular archbishop](/wiki/Titular_archbishop "Titular archbishop") of [Tarsus](/wiki/Tarsus,_Mersin "Tarsus, Mersin") in [Cilicia](/wiki/Cilicia "Cilicia") and was ordained priest (11/11/1787) and bishop (8/31/1788). After succeeding the respective bishops in Constance (1800) and Worms (1802), he also succeeded in Mainz as the last archbishop-elector, albeit temporally only in the electorate's left bank territories and also, *de facto*, in the pastoral ones as far as the right bank of the Rhine.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

As statesman, Dalberg was distinguished by his patriotic attitude, whether in ecclesiastical matters, in which he leaned to the [Febronian view](/wiki/Febronianism "Febronianism") of a German national church, or in his efforts to galvanize the atrophied machinery of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") into some sort of effective central government of [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). Failing in this, he turned to the rising star of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), believing that he had found in him the only force strong enough to save Germany from dissolution.[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1)

By the [Treaty of Lunéville](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lun%C3%A9ville "Treaty of Lunéville") in 1801, in which all territories on the left bank of the River [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") were ceded to [France](/wiki/French_First_Empire "French First Empire"), Dalberg's predecessor had to surrender Mainz and Worms; the [Concordat of 1801](/wiki/Concordat_of_1801 "Concordat of 1801") had reduced Mainz to a simple diocese in the province of [Mechelen](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Mechelen "Archdiocese of Mechelen") that conscribed the French [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Mont-Tonnerre](/wiki/Mont-Tonnerre "Mont-Tonnerre") (including the city of Worms). For Mainz, [Joseph Ludwig Colmar](/wiki/Joseph_Ludwig_Colmar "Joseph Ludwig Colmar") was soon appointed as bishop. (Worms, though it had lost its city, remained an extant diocese on the right bank of the Rhine, so Dalberg could succeed there.)

In the [Final Recess of the Extraordinary Imperial Deputation](/wiki/Reichsdeputationshauptschluss "Reichsdeputationshauptschluss") of 1803, it was decided to compensate German princes for their losses to France by distributing the church land among them, so Dalberg lost a couple of territories there (among other things, Constance), though (due to his prominent position of the Arch-Chancellor of the Empire,[[2]](#cite_note-Bischof-2) and perhaps also due to his personality and skilled diplomacy), he would be the only spiritual prince to retain at least some territory for temporal government: the Mainzian lands around [Aschaffenburg](/wiki/Principality_of_Aschaffenburg "Principality of Aschaffenburg"), the **Reichsstadt** ([Free Imperial City](/wiki/Free_Imperial_City "Free Imperial City")) of [Wetzlar](/wiki/Wetzlar "Wetzlar") (with the rank of a Countship) and the [Principality of Regensburg](/wiki/Principality_of_Regensburg "Principality of Regensburg") containing the Imperial City, the [prince-bishopric](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Regensburg "Prince-Bishopric of Regensburg"), and some independent monasteries. (Regensburg was also where the Imperial Deputation had taken place.) In addition, he was designated Archbishop of the (former [Salzburg](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Salzburg "Archdiocese of Salzburg") suffragan) Regensburg,[[4]](#cite_note-tbm-4) to which (spiritually now) the former Mainz lands on the right bank of the Rhine, and the former Mainzian suffragans were attached.

This was, of course, the decision of a state authority which, in its spiritual part, could not take effect until ratified by the Pope; in any case, Regensburg's bishop, Josef Conrad of Schroffenberg-Mös, was still alive at the time. So, Dalberg did not exercise spiritual authority in the older part of the Regensburg diocese until Bishop Schroffenberg died, at which point he made himself elected [vicar capitular](/wiki/Vicar_capitular "Vicar capitular") of the diocese; finally, on 1 February 1805, he received the papal assent and was Archbishop of Regensburg.

## Prince-Primate of the Confederation of the Rhine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karl_Theodor_Anton_Maria_von_Dalberg&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prince-Primate of the Confederation of the Rhine")]

See also: [Prince-primate § Germany - Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Prince-primate#Germany_-_Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Prince-primate")

After the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire in 1806, Dalberg together with other princes joined the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine"). He formally resigned the office of Arch-Chancellor in a letter to Emperor [Francis II](/wiki/Francis_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor"), and was appointed [prince-primate](/wiki/Prince-primate "Prince-primate") of the Confederation of the Rhine by Napoleon.[[4]](#cite_note-tbm-4) At that point, the [**Reichsstadt** of Frankfurt](/wiki/Free_City_of_Frankfurt "Free City of Frankfurt") was included among his territories. Not long after, Dalberg appointed Napoleon's uncle, [Cardinal Fesch](/wiki/Cardinal_Fesch "Cardinal Fesch"), coadjutor in his archdiocese (an action for which he had no canonical rights).

After the [Treaty of Schönbrunn](/wiki/Treaty_of_Sch%C3%B6nbrunn "Treaty of Schönbrunn") (1810), he was elevated by the French to the rank of [Grand Duke of Frankfurt](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Frankfurt "Grand Duchy of Frankfurt").[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1) This greatly augmented Dalberg's territories, although he had to cede [Regensburg](/wiki/Regensburg "Regensburg") to the [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"). As Grand Duke of Frankfurt he ordered all restrictions on the [Jews of Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurter_Judengasse "Frankfurter Judengasse") lifted. This was opposed by the Lutheran town council, until 1811, when Dalberg issued a proclamation ending the requirement that Jews live in the ghetto or pay special taxes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 14 January 1806 he performed the wedding of [Eugène de Beauharnais](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_de_Beauharnais "Eugène de Beauharnais"), Napoleon's stepson, and the Bavarian [Princess Augusta of Bavaria](/wiki/Princess_Augusta_of_Bavaria "Princess Augusta of Bavaria"). In 1813 he ceded all his temporal offices (about to be overrun by the [Sixth Coalition](/wiki/Sixth_Coalition "Sixth Coalition")) to Beauharnais.[[3]](#cite_note-Galway-3)

## Death and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Karl_Theodor_Anton_Maria_von_Dalberg&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Death and legacy")]

Dalberg died in 1817 in Regensburg. Although his political subservience to Napoleon was resented by a later generation in Germany, as a man and prelate he is remembered as amiable, conscientious and large-hearted. Himself a scholar and author, Dalberg was a notable patron of letters, and was the friend of [Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe"), [Schiller](/wiki/Friedrich_Schiller "Friedrich Schiller") and [Wieland](/wiki/Christoph_Martin_Wieland "Christoph Martin Wieland").[[1]](#cite_note-EB1911-1)
